---
title: "Lecture 13: Identity, Access, and the Zero-Trust Cloud"
---

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## readme.txt — Lecture Objectives

-   In early IT, security was a "Castle and Moat." Once inside the university VPN or corporate building, the network trusted you.
-   The cloud operates on a **Zero-Trust** model: no human, code, or network request is trusted by default.
-   Today, we will explore the **shared responsibility** model, how Google Cloud manages identities, why we use robotic service accounts, and how to place a secure login portal in front of an application.
-   **The Core Question:** Where is authorization actually enforced, and how does a system prove its identity at each boundary?
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 1: The Shared Responsibility Model

In an on-premises server room, you secure everything: physical locks, hard drives, firewalls, and code. In the cloud, security becomes a **shared responsibility**.

-   **Google's Responsibility (Lower Layers):** Physical data center security, hardware integrity, network infrastructure, and default disk encryption.
-   **Your Responsibility (Upper Layers):** Securing the data, controlling access, configuring firewalls, and writing secure application code.

Google provides the tools (IAM, firewalls), but *you* must configure them correctly.

![The shared responsibility model for different categories. ](images/shared_responsibilities.svg){fig-align="center"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## The Core Security Questions
In cloud computing, security is not just about "locking the front door." Always ask:

1.  **Who** is making the request? (Identity)
2.  **What** are they trying to do? (Role/Permission)
3.  **How** are they proving who they are? (Credential)
4.  **Where** is that decision actually enforced? (Boundary)
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 2: Cloud Identity & The Resource Hierarchy

To manage access, we first have to understand how Google Cloud organizes its assets and users.

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Cloud Identity vs. Personal Gmail
Inviting personal `@gmail.com` accounts works for small projects, but is an organizational nightmare. **Cloud Identity** allows central management (e.g., tying into UMontana's Active Directory). If an employee leaves, IT disables their account once, instantly revoking all cloud access.
:::

### 2.1 The Resource Hierarchy

Permissions cascade downward. If granted access at a high level, you inherit it for everything beneath it.

1.  **Organization:** The root node (e.g., `umontana.edu`). *(Note: Personal Gmail projects skip this and start at the Project level.)*
2.  **Folders:** Used to isolate departments (e.g., `Engineering` vs. `HR`).
3.  **Projects:** The core organizing and billing entity. Every resource belongs to a project.
4.  **Resources:** The individual services (e.g., a Compute Engine VM, a Storage bucket).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 3: The Core of IAM

IAM (Identity and Access Management) answers one specific question: **Who can do what, on which resource?**

-   **Identities ("Who"):** A Google Account, Google Group, Cloud Identity domain, or Service Account.
-   **Roles ("What"):** A collection of specific permissions (e.g., `compute.instances.start`).

::: {.callout-warning collapse="false"}
## Basic Roles (Avoid When Possible)
**Owner**, **Editor**, **Viewer**. These are dangerously broad. An `Editor` can modify almost anything in a project, breaking the principle of least privilege.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Predefined Roles (The Industry Standard)
Specific, tailored sets of permissions (e.g., `roles/compute.instanceAdmin`). This grants exactly what is needed to perform a job without sweeping project-wide access.
:::

### 3.1 Translating Theory to the Command Line

When you run policy binding commands to fix a "Permission Denied" error, you are literally writing IAM logic. Notice how this command maps to **Where, Who, and What**:

``` bash
# 1. WHERE (The resource level - here, the specific secret)
gcloud secrets add-iam-policy-binding game-db-pass \

# 2. WHO (The identity - our robotic App Engine badge)
    --member="serviceAccount:[PROJECT_ID]@appspot.gserviceaccount.com" \

# 3. WHAT (The role - the exact predefined permissions needed)
    --role="roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor"
```

### 3.2 Interactive IAM Policy Evaluator

Build an IAM policy below. Select **Who** gets access, **Where** it is granted, and **What** role is used to watch how permissions cascade.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof identity = Inputs.radio(["Alice (Human Developer)", "App-Engine-SA (Robot Identity)"], {label: "1. WHO (Identity):", value: "Alice (Human Developer)"})
viewof resourceLevel = Inputs.select(["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Cloud Storage Bucket (Low Level)", "Compute Engine VM (Low Level)"], {label: "2. WHERE (Hierarchy Level):", value: "Engineering Folder (High Level)"})
viewof role = Inputs.select(["None (Default)", "Storage Object Viewer (Predefined)", "Compute Instance Admin (Predefined)", "Project Editor (Basic / Broad)", "Explicit Deny All"], {label: "3. WHAT (Role):", value: "None (Default)"})

bucketAccess = {
  if (role === "Explicit Deny All" && ["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Cloud Storage Bucket (Low Level)"].includes(resourceLevel)) return "Blocked (Explicit Deny)";
  if (role === "None (Default)") return "No Access (Implicit Deny)";
  if (role === "Project Editor (Basic / Broad)" && ["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Cloud Storage Bucket (Low Level)"].includes(resourceLevel)) return "Read / Write / Delete";
  if (role === "Storage Object Viewer (Predefined)" && ["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Cloud Storage Bucket (Low Level)"].includes(resourceLevel)) return "Read-Only";
  return "No Access (Role not applicable or applied elsewhere)";
}

vmAccess = {
  if (role === "Explicit Deny All" && ["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Compute Engine VM (Low Level)"].includes(resourceLevel)) return "Blocked (Explicit Deny)";
  if (role === "None (Default)") return "No Access (Implicit Deny)";
  if (role === "Project Editor (Basic / Broad)" && ["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Compute Engine VM (Low Level)"].includes(resourceLevel)) return "Full VM Control";
  if (role === "Compute Instance Admin (Predefined)" && ["Engineering Folder (High Level)", "Project Alpha (Mid Level)", "Compute Engine VM (Low Level)"].includes(resourceLevel)) return "Start / Stop / Modify";
  return "No Access (Role not applicable or applied elsewhere)";
}

getColor = (access) => access.includes("Blocked") ? "#d32f2f" : access.includes("No Access") ? "#757575" : "#388e3c"

html`
<div style="border: 2px solid #1a73e8; border-radius: 8px; padding: 20px; background-color: #f8f9fa; font-family: sans-serif;">
  <h3 style="margin-top: 0; color: #1a73e8;">Effective Permissions Dashboard</h3>
  <div style="display: flex; gap: 20px; flex-wrap: wrap;">
    <div style="flex: 1; padding: 15px; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 5px; background: white;">
      <h5 style="margin-top: 0;">🪣 Cloud Storage Bucket</h5>
      <span style="color: white; padding: 6px 12px; border-radius: 20px; font-weight: 600; background-color: ${getColor(bucketAccess)}; display: inline-block;">${bucketAccess}</span>
    </div>
    <div style="flex: 1; padding: 15px; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 5px; background: white;">
      <h5 style="margin-top: 0;">💻 Compute Engine VM</h5>
      <span style="color: white; padding: 6px 12px; border-radius: 20px; font-weight: 600; background-color: ${getColor(vmAccess)}; display: inline-block;">${vmAccess}</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
`
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 4: Credentials and Trust Boundaries

**How does a system actually prove that it is who it claims to be?** It presents a credential.

-   **Identity:** *Who* is making the request (e.g., `alice@example.com`).
-   **Credential:** The *mathematical proof* presented (e.g., a password, a signed token).
-   **Authentication (AuthN):** Validating the credential.
-   **Authorization (AuthZ):** Checking IAM to see what that identity is allowed to do.

### 4.1 HTTPS vs. JWTs (The Armored Truck and the ID Badge)

Because both involve cryptography, it is easy to confuse network security with identity security.

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## The Analogy
* **HTTPS is the Armored Truck:** It creates an encrypted tunnel so hackers cannot read your data in transit. However, HTTPS has a blind spot: **it doesn't know who is inside the truck.**
* **The Token (JWT) is the ID Badge:** It is the document *inside* the truck that proves who you are to the destination server. However, its blind spot is that the data is usually readable.

**You need both.** A VIP badge is useless if you wave it around in a dangerous public street (Token without HTTPS), and a secure armored truck won't get you into a private club if you don't have ID (HTTPS without Token).
:::

### 4.2 JSON Web Tokens (JWTs)

The standard cloud credential is a short-lived **JSON Web Token (JWT)** (`xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz`). 

1.  **Header (`xxxxx`):** Declares the token type and signing algorithm.
2.  **Payload (`yyyyy`):** The data (e.g., User ID, expiration timestamp). **This is Base64 encoded, not encrypted!** Never put sensitive secrets here.
3.  **Signature (`zzzzz`):** A cryptographic seal created using a **Secret Key** known only to the server.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Catching Hackers with Math
If a hacker alters the readable payload, they cannot forge a new signature without the server's Secret Key. When the server receives the token, it recalculates the signature using its private key and the tampered payload. The math will not match, and the request is instantly rejected.
:::

### 4.3 Interactive JWT Signature Simulator

See how the Server's Secret Key prevents bad actors from elevating their permissions. Try simulating a Hacker Attack!

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof originalRole = Inputs.radio(["user", "viewer"], {label: "1. Server Issues Token with Role:", value: "user"})
viewof secretKey = Inputs.text({label: "2. Server's Secret Key (Never Shared):", value: "my-secret-key"})
viewof tamperToggle = Inputs.toggle({label: "3. Hacker Attack: Change Payload Role to 'admin'?"})

b64Header = "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9"
b64PayloadOriginal = originalRole === "user" ? "eyJzdWIiOiIxMjMiLCJyb2xlIjoidXNlciJ9" : "eyJzdWIiOiIxMjMiLCJyb2xlIjoidmlld2VyIn0"
b64PayloadTampered = "eyJzdWIiOiIxMjMiLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4ifQ"
presentedPayload = tamperToggle ? b64PayloadTampered : b64PayloadOriginal

generateMockHash = (roleStr, secretStr) => {
  if (!secretStr) return "INVALID_MISSING_SECRET";
  let combined = roleStr + secretStr;
  let hash = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < combined.length; i++) { hash = (hash << 5) - hash + combined.charCodeAt(i); hash |= 0; }
  return Math.abs(hash).toString(16) + "a9f";
}

originalSignature = generateMockHash(originalRole, secretKey)
presentedSignature = originalSignature
serverDetectedRole = tamperToggle ? "admin" : originalRole;
calculatedSignature = generateMockHash(serverDetectedRole, secretKey)

isValid = (presentedSignature === calculatedSignature) && secretKey.length > 0;
statusColor = isValid ? "#188038" : "#d93025"
statusText = isValid ? "✅ VALID: Signatures Match. Access Granted." : "❌ INVALID: Signature Mismatch. Tampering Detected!"

html`
<div style="border: 2px solid ${statusColor}; border-radius: 8px; padding: 20px; background-color: white; font-family: sans-serif;">
  <h3 style="margin-top: 0; color: ${statusColor};">JWT Verification Engine</h3>
  <div style="display: flex; gap: 20px; flex-wrap: wrap;">
    <div style="flex: 1; padding: 15px; border: 1px dashed #ccc; border-radius: 5px;">
      <strong>Attached Signature:</strong><br><small style="color: #555;">What the client sent</small><br>
      <span style="font-family: monospace; color: #188038; font-size: 1.1em;">= ${presentedSignature}</span>
    </div>
    <div style="flex: 1; padding: 15px; border: 1px dashed #ccc; border-radius: 5px;">
      <strong>Server Math (Recalculating):</strong><br><small style="color: #555;">Hash( Payload + Secret Key )</small><br>
      <span style="font-family: monospace; color: ${isValid ? '#188038' : '#d93025'}; font-size: 1.1em;">= ${calculatedSignature}</span>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div style="margin-top: 15px; padding: 12px; background: ${statusColor}; color: white; font-weight: bold; text-align: center; border-radius: 5px;">
    ${statusText}
  </div>
</div>
`
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 5: Service Accounts Deep Dive

**Service Accounts** are "ID badges for robots." 

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Application Default Credentials (ADC)
When your Python code connects to Firestore, it doesn't need a hardcoded password. Google Cloud injects short-lived tokens automatically through the environment via ADC. Your code uses this token to prove it is the `App Engine Default Service Account`.
:::

**A Service Account is both an Identity AND a Resource.**

- **As an Identity:** You grant it a role (e.g., *"The Robot can read the bucket"*).
- **As a Resource:** It has its own IAM policy. You grant a human permission to *use* it (e.g., *"Alice can impersonate the Robot"*).

::: {.callout-warning collapse="false"}
## Avoid Key Files!
Whenever possible, attach a Service Account directly to your workload and let ADC handle the tokens. Avoid downloading long-lived JSON key files; if uploaded to GitHub, hackers can use them forever.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 6: Where Authorization Is Actually Enforced

Authorization is not enforced in the same place for every app. Always ask: **"What kind of app are we building, and where does the access decision actually live?"**

### Server-Side Application (App Engine + Python)

- The browser talks to your backend, and your backend talks to Google Cloud. 
- **Backend code** handles application-level authorization.
- **IAM** governs what the backend's Service Account can access.

### Client-Side Application (Firebase Web SDK)

- The browser talks directly to the database without a backend server.
- **Firestore Security Rules** evaluate the user's token directly at the data layer.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 7: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)

**Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)** provides a modern, Zero-Trust alternative to a VPN. It sits directly in front of your App Engine service, intercepts incoming traffic, forces Google authentication, and checks IAM to ensure the user is on the VIP list.

::: {.callout-important collapse="false"}
## The Front Door
IAP is an **application-boundary access control layer**. It keeps unauthorized humans out. However, once an authorized user gets past IAP, your backend code must still decide: *Which specific database records should this user be allowed to see?*
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 8: Interactive Credential Flow Explorer

Explore how identity changes across trust boundaries. Notice what credential is presented, who validates it, and where authorization is actually enforced.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
flows = ({
  "Server-side App (IAP + App Engine + Firestore)": {
    stages: [
      { label: "1. Human User -> Google Login", credential: "MFA / Session", verifier: "Google Identity", identity: "alice@example.com (human)", authz: "Authentication only" },
      { label: "2. Google / IAP -> App Engine", credential: "IAP signed assertion", verifier: "IAP", identity: "App learns user is Alice", authz: "IAP decides if Alice can reach the app" },
      { label: "3. App Engine -> Firestore", credential: "ADC / Service Account Token", verifier: "Google Cloud API", identity: "my-app@appspot.gserviceaccount.com (workload)", authz: "IAM evaluates the service account" }
    ],
    footer: "Firestore sees the service account, not Alice directly. Authorization lives in App Code + IAM."
  },
  "Client-side App (Firebase Auth + Firestore)": {
    stages: [
      { label: "1. Human User -> Firebase Auth", credential: "OAuth / Password", verifier: "Identity Provider", identity: "alice@example.com (human)", authz: "Authentication only" },
      { label: "2. Browser -> Firestore", credential: "User JWT", verifier: "Firestore", identity: "Firestore sees Alice directly", authz: "Firestore Security Rules evaluate request.auth" }
    ],
    footer: "Firestore sees Alice directly. Authorization lives in Security Rules."
  }
})

viewof selectedFlow = Inputs.select(Object.keys(flows), {label: "Choose Architecture:"})
currentFlow = flows[selectedFlow]

html`
<div style="border: 2px solid #1a73e8; border-radius: 10px; padding: 20px; background: #f8f9fa; font-family: sans-serif;">
  <div style="display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 16px;">
    ${currentFlow.stages.map((stage, i) => html`
      <div style="flex: 1; min-width: 200px; background: white; border: 2px solid #1a73e8; border-radius: 8px;">
        <div style="background: #1a73e8; color: white; padding: 8px 12px; font-weight: bold;">${stage.label}</div>
        <div style="padding: 12px; font-size: 0.9em;">
          <strong>Credential:</strong> ${stage.credential}<br>
          <strong>Identity:</strong> ${stage.identity}<br>
          <strong>AuthZ:</strong> ${stage.authz}
        </div>
      </div>
    `)}
  </div>
  <div style="margin-top: 15px; padding: 12px; background: #e8f0fe; border-left: 5px solid #1a73e8;"><strong>Key Takeaway:</strong> ${currentFlow.footer}</div>
</div>
`
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part 9: Looking Ahead

We are now ready for a nuanced view of cloud security:

-   **IAM** controls access between identities and resources.
-   **Credentials** (like JWTs) prove identity mathematically.
-   **Service Accounts** give backend code a passwordless identity.
-   **IAP** protects the front door.

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Preview of the Next Lab
We will combine these concepts to build a multi-user application. IAP will act as the bouncer, our Python backend will enforce data visibility, and our Service Account will securely ferry data to Firestore!
:::
